name: jc — JumpCloud CLI
description: A modern, LLM-friendly CLI for JumpCloud written in Go. Covers the full JumpCloud API surface (v1, v2, Directory Insights) with built-in MCP server support, recipe system, plan mode, and conversational interface. Targets macOS/Linux administrators who need native CLI tooling for JumpCloud management.

userStories.title: Go Module Setup with Cobra and Viper
userStories.description: As a developer, I want the Go project scaffolded with module initialization, Cobra for command routing, and Viper for configuration binding so that all subsequent commands and config features build on a solid foundation.
acceptanceCriteria: Go module initialized with appropriate module path (e.g., github.com/klaassen-consulting/jc)
Cobra root command (jc) created with version, help, and completion subcommands
Viper integrated for config file reading and environment variable binding
Project structure follows Go standard layout: cmd/, internal/, pkg/ directories
Makefile or Taskfile with build, test, lint, and install targets
go.sum committed with all dependencies resolved
Running 'go build ./...' succeeds with zero errors
Running 'jc --version' prints version string (e.g., jc v0.1.0-dev)
Running 'jc --help' prints root help with command groups listed
userStories.notes: Use Go 1.22+. Structure: cmd/jc/main.go as entry point, internal/cmd/ for Cobra commands, internal/config/ for Viper setup. Consider using goreleaser config for future releases.

userStories.title: Configuration File System
userStories.description: As an administrator, I want jc to read configuration from ~/.config/jc/config.yaml so that I can persist defaults like output format, pagination limits, and active profile without repeating flags.
acceptanceCriteria: Config file loaded from ~/.config/jc/config.yaml by default
JC_CONFIG environment variable overrides config file path
Config file auto-created with sensible defaults on first run if missing
Config directory created with 0700 permissions, config file with 0600
Config supports 'defaults' section with output, limit, confirm_destructive, color, pager fields
Config supports 'cache' section with enabled, ttl, directory fields
Config supports 'profiles' section (map of named profiles)
Config supports 'active_profile' top-level field
Invalid YAML produces clear error message with line number
Config values accessible throughout the application via Viper
userStories.notes: Use XDG Base Directory spec. Viper handles YAML natively. Ensure config file permissions are restrictive since it may reference secrets.

userStories.title: Environment Variable Support
userStories.description: As an administrator, I want jc to read configuration from environment variables so that I can configure behavior in CI/CD pipelines, containers, and scripts without config files.
acceptanceCriteria: JC_API_KEY sets the API key (highest priority over config file)
JC_ORG_ID sets the organization ID
JC_PROFILE selects the active profile
JC_OUTPUT sets the default output format
JC_CONFIG overrides the config file path
JC_NO_COLOR disables color output when set to any non-empty value
Environment variables take priority over config file values
Command-line flags take priority over environment variables
Priority chain verified: flags > env vars > config file > built-in defaults
Running with JC_OUTPUT=table produces table output without --output flag
userStories.notes: Viper supports env var binding natively. Use JC_ prefix for all env vars. Document all env vars in --help output.

userStories.title: API Key Authentication from Config and Environment
userStories.description: As an administrator, I want to authenticate to JumpCloud using an API key provided via environment variable or config file so that all API calls are authenticated.
acceptanceCriteria: API key read from JC_API_KEY environment variable (highest priority)
API key read from active profile in config file if env var not set
API key injected as x-api-key header on all API requests
Missing API key produces clear error: 'No API key configured. Run jc auth login or set JC_API_KEY'
API key validated on first use by calling GET /api/organizations
Invalid API key produces clear error with suggestion to re-authenticate
--api-key flag allows one-off override for a single command
API key never appears in --verbose or --debug output (redacted to last 4 chars)
userStories.notes: Plaintext api_key field in config is allowed for this story as a fallback; keychain integration comes in US-005. Always redact API keys in logs.

userStories.title: OS Keychain Integration for Secret Storage
userStories.description: As a security-conscious administrator, I want API keys stored in the OS keychain (macOS Keychain / Linux secret-tool) so that secrets are never stored as plaintext on disk.
acceptanceCriteria: On macOS, API keys stored in Keychain via Security framework or keychain CLI
On Linux, API keys stored via secret-tool (freedesktop Secret Service)
Config file references keychain entries as 'keychain://jc/<profile-name>'
jc auth login stores the key in keychain and writes reference to config
API key retrieval from keychain works transparently for all commands
Graceful fallback to plaintext config if keychain unavailable (with warning)
jc auth logout removes the key from keychain
Keychain service name is 'jc' with account name matching profile name
userStories.notes: Use go-keyring or zalando/go-keyring package for cross-platform keychain access. Test on both macOS and Linux.

userStories.title: Auth Login and Auth Status Commands
userStories.description: As an administrator, I want jc auth login to interactively set up my API key and jc auth status to verify my authentication so that I can easily configure and verify credentials.
acceptanceCriteria: 'jc auth login' prompts for API key with masked input
'jc auth login' optionally prompts for org ID if not already set
'jc auth login' validates the API key by calling GET /api/organizations
'jc auth login' stores key in keychain and updates config profile
'jc auth login --profile <name>' creates or updates a named profile
'jc auth status' displays: authenticated (yes/no), profile name, org name, org ID, user role
'jc auth status' returns exit code 0 if authenticated, 3 if not
'jc auth logout' removes credentials from keychain and clears profile
'jc auth status --output json' returns structured JSON for scripting
userStories.notes: Use survey or huh for interactive prompts. Masked input critical for API key entry. The login flow should feel similar to 'gh auth login'.

userStories.title: Base HTTP Transport Layer
userStories.description: As a developer, I want a reusable HTTP transport layer that handles authentication injection, TLS, request/response logging, and user-agent headers so that all API clients build on consistent, secure HTTP behavior.
acceptanceCriteria: HTTP client automatically injects x-api-key header from active credentials
All requests use HTTPS (TLS 1.2+)
User-Agent header set to 'jc/<version> (Go; <os>/<arch>)'
Content-Type header set to 'application/json' for all requests
--verbose flag logs: method, URL, status code, and duration for each request
--debug flag logs: full request/response headers and body (with API key redacted)
Request timeout configurable (default 30 seconds)
Connection pooling enabled for multiple sequential requests
Accept header set to 'application/json'
userStories.notes: Use net/http with a custom RoundTripper for auth injection and logging. Consider go-resty for convenience. The transport is the foundation for both V1 and V2 API clients.

userStories.title: V1 API Client with Pagination, Rate Limiting, and Retry
userStories.description: As a developer, I want a V1 API client that handles automatic pagination, rate limit detection with backoff, and retry logic so that all V1 resource commands work reliably against large datasets.
acceptanceCriteria: V1 API base URL: https://console.jumpcloud.com/api
Automatic pagination: list endpoints iterate through all pages transparently
Pagination uses skip/limit parameters for V1 endpoints
Rate limit detection: HTTP 429 triggers exponential backoff with jitter
Retry logic: up to 3 retries for transient errors (429, 500, 502, 503, 504)
Error mapping: HTTP status codes mapped to structured error types
--limit flag caps total results returned (stops pagination early)
--page-size flag controls per-request page size (default 100)
API errors include HTTP status, endpoint, and JumpCloud error message
Context cancellation (Ctrl+C) stops in-flight pagination immediately
userStories.notes: V1 API uses skip-based pagination. Rate limit is 200 requests/minute per API key. Backoff formula: min(2^attempt * 1s + jitter, 30s).

userStories.title: Output Engine with JSON, Table, and CSV Formatters
userStories.description: As an administrator, I want command output in JSON (default), table, or CSV format so that I can pipe results to other tools, read them visually, or import into spreadsheets.
acceptanceCriteria: JSON output (default): pretty-printed with 2-space indent to stdout
JSON output: list commands always produce arrays (even for single results or empty)
JSON output: single-resource commands produce objects (not arrays)
JSON output: field order is consistent and alphabetical
JSON output: timestamps in RFC 3339 UTC, booleans as true/false, nulls explicit
Table output (--output table or -t): ASCII table with column headers and alignment
Table output: long values truncated with ellipsis, columns auto-sized to terminal width
CSV output (--output csv): standard CSV with header row, proper escaping
Human output (--output human or -h): key-value pairs for single resources
--quiet flag (-q): suppresses all output, exit code only
--ids flag: outputs one ID per line (for piping to other jc commands)
Output format selectable via --output flag, JC_OUTPUT env var, or config default
userStories.notes: Use tablewriter for ASCII tables. JSON is the LLM-friendly default. Table is for human scanning. CSV for export. The output engine should be a shared package used by all commands.

userStories.title: Global Flags Framework
userStories.description: As an administrator, I want consistent global flags across all commands (--output, --filter, --limit, --sort, --fields, --verbose, --debug, --org, --force, --plan) so that the CLI behaves predictably regardless of which resource I'm working with.
acceptanceCriteria: --output / -o flag registered on root command, inherited by all subcommands
--verbose / -v flag enables HTTP request/response logging
--debug flag enables full debug logging
--quiet / -q flag suppresses output
--force / -f flag skips confirmation prompts
--non-interactive flag disables all interactive prompts
--org flag overrides active profile for one command
--no-cache flag bypasses name-to-ID cache
--plan flag registered as persistent flag (available on all mutating commands)
Flags correctly bound to Viper for env var and config file override
Unknown flags produce clear error with suggestion of similar valid flags
Short flags (-o, -t, -q, -v, -f, -V) work correctly
userStories.notes: Use Cobra persistent flags on root command for truly global flags. Resource-specific flags (--filter, --limit, --sort, --fields) will be added per-command in later stories but the pattern should be established here.

userStories.title: Users List and Get Commands
userStories.description: As an administrator, I want to list all users and get a specific user by username or ID so that I can view my JumpCloud user directory from the command line.
acceptanceCriteria: 'jc users list' returns all system users as a JSON array
'jc users list' shows default field subset: username, email, firstname, lastname, activated, suspended
'jc users list --output table' renders a readable ASCII table
'jc users list --limit 10' returns at most 10 users
'jc users list --sort username' sorts by username ascending
'jc users list --sort -created' sorts by created descending
'jc users get <username>' returns a single user JSON object by username
'jc users get <id>' returns a single user JSON object by ID
User not found returns exit code 1 with helpful error message
List footer shows '── N of TOTAL items ──' count information
V1 API endpoint: GET /api/systemusers used correctly
userStories.notes: V1 System Users API: GET /api/systemusers (list), GET /api/systemusers/{id} (get). Name resolution (username → ID) will be handled in US-018.

userStories.title: Users Create, Update, and Delete Commands
userStories.description: As an administrator, I want to create, update, and delete JumpCloud users from the CLI so that I can manage the user lifecycle without the web console.
acceptanceCriteria: 'jc users create --username jdoe --email jdoe@acme.com --firstname John --lastname Doe' creates a user
Required fields validated before API call: username and email
Create returns the newly created user object
'jc users update <user> --department Sales --jobTitle Manager' updates specified fields only
Update returns the updated user object showing new values
'jc users delete <user>' deletes the user after confirmation prompt
'jc users delete <user> --force' deletes without confirmation
Delete returns exit code 0 on success with brief confirmation message
Deleting a non-existent user returns clear error
V1 API endpoints used: POST /api/systemusers, PUT /api/systemusers/{id}, DELETE /api/systemusers/{id}
userStories.notes: Confirmation prompt for delete should show username and email. Update should accept any valid user field as a flag.

userStories.title: Users Search Command
userStories.description: As an administrator, I want to search for users by keyword so that I can quickly find users when I don't know their exact username.
acceptanceCriteria: 'jc users search <term>' searches across username, email, firstname, lastname fields
Search uses the V1 POST /api/search/systemusers endpoint
Results returned as JSON array matching standard list format
Search supports --output table for visual scanning
Empty results return empty array with exit code 0
Search term is case-insensitive
Results can be filtered further with --filter flag
Results can be limited with --limit flag
userStories.notes: V1 Search API uses POST with JSON body containing search filter. This is different from the list+filter approach.

userStories.title: Users Lock, Unlock, Reset-MFA, and Reset-Password
userStories.description: As an administrator, I want to lock/unlock user accounts, reset MFA enrollment, and trigger password resets so that I can handle common security operations from the CLI.
acceptanceCriteria: 'jc users lock <user>' sets account_locked=true on the user
'jc users unlock <user>' sets account_locked=false on the user
'jc users reset-mfa <user>' resets TOTP/MFA enrollment for the user
'jc users reset-password <user>' triggers a password reset email
Lock/unlock commands confirm action and show resulting account state
All four commands accept username or user ID as positional argument
Lock shows confirmation: 'User jdoe locked successfully'
Unlock shows confirmation: 'User jdoe unlocked successfully'
reset-mfa shows confirmation with warning about user re-enrollment
These commands respect --plan flag (showing what would change)
userStories.notes: Lock/unlock use PUT /api/systemusers/{id} with account_locked field. MFA reset and password reset have specific V1 endpoints.

userStories.title: Devices List and Get Commands
userStories.description: As an administrator, I want to list all devices (systems) and get a specific device by hostname or ID so that I can manage the device fleet from the CLI.
acceptanceCriteria: 'jc devices list' returns all systems as a JSON array
'jc devices list' shows default fields: displayName, hostname, os, osVersion, lastContact, agentVersion
'jc devices list --output table' renders a readable device table
'jc devices list --filter "os=Mac OS X"' filters by operating system
'jc devices list --limit 20' limits results
'jc devices list --sort -lastContact' sorts by last contact descending
'jc devices get <hostname>' returns a single device JSON object
'jc devices get <id>' returns a single device JSON object by ID
Device not found returns exit code 1 with helpful error
V1 API endpoint: GET /api/systems used correctly
userStories.notes: V1 Systems API: GET /api/systems (list), GET /api/systems/{id} (get). Hostname-to-ID resolution will use the same caching mechanism as username resolution (US-018).

userStories.title: Devices Delete Command
userStories.description: As an administrator, I want to delete a device record from JumpCloud so that I can remove decommissioned or stale systems.
acceptanceCriteria: 'jc devices delete <device>' deletes the device after confirmation prompt
Confirmation shows device hostname, OS, and last contact date
'jc devices delete <device> --force' skips confirmation
Delete returns exit code 0 with brief confirmation message
Deleting a non-existent device returns clear error
Accepts hostname or device ID as positional argument
V1 API endpoint: DELETE /api/systems/{id}
userStories.notes: Device deletion is destructive and should warn about agent removal implications.

userStories.title: Devices MDM Commands (Lock, Restart, Erase)
userStories.description: As an administrator, I want to send MDM commands to devices (lock, restart, erase) so that I can remotely manage devices for security incidents or maintenance.
acceptanceCriteria: 'jc devices lock <device>' sends MDM lock command
'jc devices restart <device>' sends MDM restart command
'jc devices erase <device>' sends MDM erase command
'jc devices erase' REQUIRES --confirm-erase flag (safety gate)
Erase without --confirm-erase shows error explaining the requirement
All MDM commands show confirmation prompt before execution
Lock shows device hostname and brief result
Commands accept hostname or device ID
Appropriate V1 API MDM endpoints used for each command
userStories.notes: Device erase is extremely destructive — the double confirmation (prompt + --confirm-erase flag) is intentional. MDM commands may not apply to all device types.

userStories.title: Name-to-ID Resolution with Caching
userStories.description: As an administrator, I want to reference users by username and devices by hostname instead of raw IDs, with results cached for performance, so that the CLI is human-friendly.
acceptanceCriteria: Users can be referenced by username (e.g., 'jc users get jdoe')
Devices can be referenced by hostname (e.g., 'jc devices get JDOE-MBP')
Resolution checks if input looks like an ID (24-char hex) and uses it directly
Non-ID input triggers a search/filter API call to resolve to ID
Resolved name→ID mappings cached in ~/.cache/jc/ with configurable TTL (default 300s)
Cache stored as JSON files (one per resource type)
--no-cache flag bypasses cache and forces fresh resolution
Cache miss triggers API lookup transparently
Ambiguous name (multiple matches) produces error listing matches
Cache directory created with 0700 permissions
userStories.notes: Cache key format: resource_type:name → id. Use file-based cache under ~/.cache/jc/. Consider bolt or badger for future, but JSON files are fine for v1.

userStories.title: Shell Completions (Bash, Zsh, Fish)
userStories.description: As an administrator, I want shell completions for commands, subcommands, flags, and resource names so that I can use tab completion for faster CLI usage.
acceptanceCriteria: 'jc completion bash' outputs Bash completion script
'jc completion zsh' outputs Zsh completion script
'jc completion fish' outputs Fish completion script
Completions include all command groups and subcommands
Completions include all global and command-specific flags
Completions include --output format values (json, table, csv, human, yaml, ndjson)
Instructions printed for adding completions to shell RC file
Cobra's built-in completion generation used as foundation
userStories.notes: Cobra generates completions natively. Dynamic completions (e.g., completing usernames) can be added later. Focus on static command/flag completions first.

userStories.title: Filtering Engine
userStories.description: As an administrator, I want to filter list results using --filter 'field=value' syntax with comparison operators so that I can narrow down results without post-processing.
acceptanceCriteria: --filter 'field=value' filters by exact equality
--filter 'field!=value' filters by inequality
--filter 'field>=value' filters by greater-than-or-equal (dates and numbers)
--filter 'field<=value' filters by less-than-or-equal
--filter 'field>value' and --filter 'field<value' for strict comparison
Multiple --filter flags combine with AND logic
Filters translated to appropriate API query parameters (V1: filter param, V2: query params)
--search flag provides full-text search across fields
Invalid filter syntax produces clear error with expected format
Filters work consistently across users, devices, and all future resource commands
userStories.notes: V1 API filter format: 'field:$eq:value', 'field:$gte:value'. V2 uses query string parameters. The filter parser should be a shared utility that translates the user-friendly syntax to API-specific format.

userStories.title: Field Selection (--fields, --exclude, --all)
userStories.description: As an administrator, I want to select which fields appear in output so that I can focus on relevant data and reduce noise.
acceptanceCriteria: --fields 'username,email,department' outputs only specified fields
--exclude 'password_date,totp_enabled' outputs all default fields except those excluded
--all includes all available fields including nested objects
Field selection works with all output formats (JSON, table, CSV)
Invalid field names produce warning but don't fail the command
Fields flag accepts comma-separated list without spaces requirement
Default field set per resource type defined (not all fields shown by default)
--fields and --exclude are mutually exclusive (error if both provided)
userStories.notes: Each resource type should define its default field set and full field set. Field selection happens client-side after API response (not all APIs support server-side field selection).

userStories.title: Config View and Set Commands
userStories.description: As an administrator, I want jc config view and jc config set commands so that I can inspect and modify configuration from the CLI without editing YAML manually.
acceptanceCriteria: 'jc config view' displays the full config (with secrets redacted)
'jc config view --output json' outputs config as JSON
'jc config view --output yaml' outputs config as YAML
'jc config set defaults.output table' sets a config value using dot notation
'jc config set defaults.limit 50' handles numeric values
'jc config set defaults.confirm_destructive true' handles boolean values
Config changes written atomically to config file
Invalid config key produces error with list of valid keys
'jc config view' shows active profile highlighted
API keys shown as '****<last4>' in config view output
userStories.notes: Use Viper's WriteConfig after updates. Atomic write via temp file + rename. Dot notation allows setting nested keys like 'defaults.output'.

userStories.title: V2 API Client Layer
userStories.description: As a developer, I want a V2 API client that handles the different pagination, header format, and endpoint structure of JumpCloud's V2 API so that group, policy, and other V2 resources can be implemented.
acceptanceCriteria: V2 API base URL: https://console.jumpcloud.com/api/v2
V2 pagination uses Link headers (next/prev) instead of skip/limit
V2 API accepts same x-api-key authentication header
V2 content-type is application/json
Rate limiting and retry logic shared with V1 client
V2 client handles query parameter-based filtering
V2 pagination automatically follows Link headers to fetch all pages
--limit flag works correctly with V2 pagination
V2 error responses parsed and mapped to structured error types
V2 client reuses the same HTTP transport layer as V1
userStories.notes: V2 API differences: pagination via Link headers, different filter syntax, different endpoint paths. The V2 client should share the transport layer from US-007 but have its own pagination logic.

userStories.title: User Groups CRUD Commands
userStories.description: As an administrator, I want to list, get, create, update, and delete user groups so that I can manage group-based access policies from the CLI.
acceptanceCriteria: 'jc groups user list' lists all user groups
'jc groups user get <group-name-or-id>' returns a single user group
'jc groups user create --name Engineering --description "Engineering team"' creates a user group
'jc groups user update <group> --description "Updated desc"' updates a group
'jc groups user delete <group>' deletes a group after confirmation
Group name-to-ID resolution works (reference groups by name)
All commands support standard output formats (JSON, table)
V2 API endpoints: /api/v2/usergroups used correctly
Delete shows group name, member count, and associated resources in confirmation
userStories.notes: V2 User Groups API: GET/POST /api/v2/usergroups, GET/PUT/DELETE /api/v2/usergroups/{id}. Group commands nest under 'jc groups user' and 'jc groups device'.

userStories.title: Device Groups CRUD Commands
userStories.description: As an administrator, I want to list, get, create, update, and delete device (system) groups so that I can manage device grouping from the CLI.
acceptanceCriteria: 'jc groups device list' lists all system groups
'jc groups device get <group-name-or-id>' returns a single system group
'jc groups device create --name "macOS Fleet" --description "All macOS devices"' creates a group
'jc groups device update <group> --name "macOS Devices"' updates a group
'jc groups device delete <group>' deletes after confirmation
Group name-to-ID resolution works for device groups
V2 API endpoints: /api/v2/systemgroups used correctly
All standard output formats supported
userStories.notes: V2 System Groups API: GET/POST /api/v2/systemgroups, GET/PUT/DELETE /api/v2/systemgroups/{id}.

userStories.title: Group Membership Management (Add/Remove Members)
userStories.description: As an administrator, I want to add and remove members from user and device groups so that I can manage group membership from the CLI.
acceptanceCriteria: 'jc groups add-member <group> --user <username>' adds a user to a user group
'jc groups add-member <group> --device <hostname>' adds a device to a device group
'jc groups remove-member <group> --user <username>' removes a user from a group
'jc groups remove-member <group> --device <hostname>' removes a device from a group
'jc groups remove-member --all --user <username>' removes user from ALL groups
Membership changes confirmed with brief message showing group and member
Adding an already-existing member returns informative message (not error)
Removing a non-member returns informative message
V2 Graph/membership API endpoints used correctly
userStories.notes: Group membership uses V2 Graph API: POST /api/v2/usergroups/{id}/members and /api/v2/systemgroups/{id}/membership.

userStories.title: Commands CRUD
userStories.description: As an administrator, I want to list, get, create, update, and delete JumpCloud commands so that I can manage remote commands from the CLI.
acceptanceCriteria: 'jc commands list' lists all commands with name, type, schedule info
'jc commands get <command-name-or-id>' returns command details
'jc commands create --name "Update Agents" --command "apt update" --type linux' creates a command
'jc commands update <command> --command "apt upgrade -y"' updates command body
'jc commands delete <command>' deletes after confirmation
Command name-to-ID resolution works
V1 API endpoints: /api/commands used correctly
Standard output formats supported
userStories.notes: V1 Commands API: GET/POST /api/commands, GET/PUT/DELETE /api/commands/{id}.

userStories.title: Commands Trigger and Results
userStories.description: As an administrator, I want to trigger command execution on devices/groups and view results so that I can run remote operations and check their outcomes.
acceptanceCriteria: 'jc commands run <command> --on <device>' triggers command on a specific device
'jc commands run <command> --on <group>' triggers command on a device group
'jc commands results <command>' lists execution results for a command
Results show: device, exit code, stdout, stderr, timestamp
Results support --output table for quick scanning
Run command confirms target count before execution
'jc commands run <command> --on <device> --force' skips confirmation
V1 API endpoints: POST /api/commands/{id}/trigger, GET /api/commandresults
userStories.notes: Command trigger API uses POST /api/runcommand. Results via GET /api/commandresults with command filter.

userStories.title: Policies List, Get, and Results
userStories.description: As an administrator, I want to list policies, get policy details, and view policy results so that I can manage and audit configuration policies from the CLI.
acceptanceCriteria: 'jc policies list' lists all policies with name, type, and OS target
'jc policies get <policy-name-or-id>' returns full policy details
'jc policies results <policy>' lists policy application results per device
Results show: device, status (applied/pending/failed), timestamp
Policy name-to-ID resolution works
V2 API endpoints: /api/v2/policies used correctly
Standard output formats and filtering supported
userStories.notes: V2 Policies API: GET /api/v2/policies, GET /api/v2/policies/{id}, GET /api/v2/policies/{id}/policystatuses.

userStories.title: Apps List, Get, and Associations
userStories.description: As an administrator, I want to list SSO applications, get app details, and view associated groups/users so that I can manage application access from the CLI.
acceptanceCriteria: 'jc apps list' lists all SSO applications with name, type, and status
'jc apps get <app-name-or-id>' returns full application details
App name-to-ID resolution works
App details include associated user groups and device groups
V1/V2 application API endpoints used correctly
Standard output formats and filtering supported
userStories.notes: Applications span V1 (GET /api/applications) and V2 graph associations.

userStories.title: Graph Association Traversal
userStories.description: As an administrator, I want to traverse JumpCloud graph associations between resources so that I can understand relationships (user→groups, device→groups, app→groups, etc.).
acceptanceCriteria: 'jc graph traverse --from user:<username> --to user_group' shows groups a user belongs to
'jc graph traverse --from device:<hostname> --to system_group' shows device group memberships
'jc graph traverse --from user_group:<group> --to application' shows apps associated with a group
Output is a list of associated resource objects with type and ID
V2 Graph API: /api/v2/{resource_type}/{id}/associations used correctly
Standard output formats supported (JSON default, table for visual)
Association types validated (error on invalid from→to combinations)
userStories.notes: V2 Graph API enables traversal between all resource types. The graph command is powerful for auditing access relationships. Support common traversals first.

userStories.title: Admins List Command
userStories.description: As an administrator, I want to list JumpCloud administrators so that I can audit admin access to the organization.
acceptanceCriteria: 'jc admins list' lists all administrators with email, role, and MFA status
Output supports standard formats (JSON, table)
Filtering by role or email supported
V2 API endpoint: /api/v2/administrators used correctly
Results include admin role (e.g., Administrator, Manager, Read Only)
userStories.notes: V2 Administrators API: GET /api/v2/administrators. Read-only for now (list/get).

userStories.title: Bulk Operations
userStories.description: As an administrator, I want bulk user operations (create, update, delete from file) so that I can manage large-scale user changes efficiently.
acceptanceCriteria: 'jc bulk users --file users.csv' processes bulk user operations from CSV
CSV format: each row is an operation (create/update/delete based on context or column)
Progress shown during bulk operations (N of TOTAL processed)
Failed operations logged but don't stop remaining operations
Summary report at end: N succeeded, N failed, N skipped
Failed operations detail available with --verbose
Bulk operations support --plan to preview all changes
'jc bulk users --file users.csv --force' skips confirmation
Rate limiting respected during bulk operations
userStories.notes: Bulk uses a combination of individual API calls with rate limiting. Consider using JumpCloud's bulk job API where available (POST /api/v2/bulk/users).

userStories.title: Plan Mode Engine
userStories.description: As an administrator, I want --plan on all mutating commands to preview what would happen before execution so that I can verify changes (especially those generated by LLMs) before they take effect.
acceptanceCriteria: 'jc users delete jdoe --plan' shows plan without executing
Plan displays: action, target (with resolved name + ID), and effects
Plan for delete shows group memberships and device bindings that would be removed
Plan for update shows field changes as 'field: old_value → new_value'
Plan for create shows all fields that would be set
'--plan --output json' returns structured plan JSON with action, resource, target, effects, reversible
Plan mode makes read-only API calls only (never modifies)
Plan exit code is 10 (no changes made, distinct from success)
Plan mode renders a visual box in terminal for human consumption
Plan mode works on: users create/update/delete/lock/unlock, devices delete/lock/erase, groups create/update/delete/add-member/remove-member
userStories.notes: Plan mode is critical for LLM safety — it lets agents preview commands before execution. Plan JSON format must be deterministic for machine consumption. Visual box uses lipgloss/glamour.

userStories.title: Multi-Org Profile Switching
userStories.description: As an MSP administrator managing multiple JumpCloud organizations, I want to switch between org profiles so that I can manage all clients from a single CLI installation.
acceptanceCriteria: 'jc auth switch <profile-name>' switches the active profile
'jc auth switch' without argument shows interactive profile picker
Profile switch updates active_profile in config file
Each profile has its own org_id, API key reference, and optional defaults
'jc --org <profile>' allows one-off override without switching
'jc auth status' shows current active profile and org name
Tab completion works for profile names
Non-existent profile name produces error with list of available profiles
userStories.notes: Profile switching is essential for MSP workflows. The --org flag is a per-command override that doesn't persist. Each profile can have different auth methods.

userStories.title: OAuth 2.0 Service Account Authentication
userStories.description: As an administrator, I want to authenticate using OAuth 2.0 service accounts (client ID + client secret) so that I can use scoped, rotatable credentials for automation.
acceptanceCriteria: 'jc auth login --service-account' prompts for client ID and client secret
Service account credentials exchange for a bearer token via JumpCloud OAuth endpoint
Bearer token cached and auto-refreshed on expiry
Config profile stores auth_method: service_account, client_id, and client_secret_ref (keychain)
Bearer token injected as Authorization header instead of x-api-key
Token refresh happens transparently before API calls
'jc auth status' shows auth method as 'service_account' with token expiry info
Expired token with failed refresh produces clear re-authentication message
userStories.notes: OAuth 2.0 is JumpCloud's newer auth method. Service accounts provide scoped access and credential rotation. Client secret stored in keychain like API keys.

userStories.title: Directory Insights API Client
userStories.description: As a developer, I want a Directory Insights API client that handles the event query POST endpoints so that insight commands can query audit and activity events.
acceptanceCriteria: Client targets POST /insights/directory/v1/events endpoint
Client targets POST /insights/directory/v1/events/count endpoint
Client targets POST /insights/directory/v1/events/distinct endpoint
Request body includes: service, start_time, end_time, fields, filter, limit, skip, sort
Timestamps parsed from human-friendly formats: '2026-02-01', 'last 24h', 'last 7d', 'last 30d'
Multiple services supported as comma-separated list
Pagination handled automatically for large result sets
Rate limiting and retry logic applied consistently
API base URL: https://console.jumpcloud.com/insights/directory/v1
userStories.notes: Directory Insights uses a POST-based query API (not GET). The request body contains query parameters. Time parsing from '24h', '7d', '30d' shortcuts is a nice UX feature.

userStories.title: Insights Query Command
userStories.description: As an administrator, I want to query Directory Insights events so that I can audit user activity, authentication events, and system changes.
acceptanceCriteria: 'jc insights query --service sso --last 24h' returns SSO events from the last 24 hours
'jc insights query --service sso,ldap --start 2026-02-01 --end 2026-02-13' queries date range
'jc insights query --service all --last 7d' queries all services
'jc insights query --service sso --event-type sso_auth_failed --last 24h' filters by event type
Results returned as JSON array by default
Results support table output for scanning
--limit flag controls maximum events returned
Event fields include: timestamp, event_type, initiated_by, client_ip, success
Service categories validated: sso, radius, ldap, user_portal, admin, mdm, etc.
Invalid service name produces error with list of valid services
userStories.notes: Directory Insights is JumpCloud's audit/event API. Services map to event sources. The --last shortcut is ergonomic for common time ranges.

userStories.title: Insights Count and Distinct Commands
userStories.description: As an administrator, I want to count events and get distinct field values so that I can quickly aggregate audit data without retrieving full event records.
acceptanceCriteria: 'jc insights count --service sso --last 7d' returns event count
'jc insights count --service sso --event-type sso_auth_failed --last 24h' counts filtered events
'jc insights distinct --service sso --field initiated_by.username --last 30d' returns unique values
Count returns a single number (or JSON object with count field)
Distinct returns an array of unique values
Both commands support the same --service, --last, --start, --end, --event-type flags
Standard output formats supported
userStories.notes: Count and distinct are lightweight aggregation queries. Distinct is useful for finding unique actors, IP addresses, event types.

userStories.title: Saved Searches for Insights
userStories.description: As an administrator, I want to save, list, and re-run frequently used insight queries so that I can quickly execute common audit searches.
acceptanceCriteria: 'jc insights save "failed-sso-24h" --service sso --event-type sso_auth_failed --last 24h' saves a search
'jc insights run "failed-sso-24h"' executes a saved search
'jc insights saved' lists all saved searches with name and description
Saved searches stored in config file under 'insights.saved_searches' key
Saved search captures all query parameters (service, time range, event type, filters)
'jc insights saved --output json' returns saved searches as JSON
Time-relative searches (--last 24h) re-calculate from current time when re-run
Non-existent saved search name produces error with list of available searches
userStories.notes: Saved searches are local to the config file. Time-relative queries (--last 24h) are recalculated each run, not stored as absolute timestamps.

userStories.title: NDJSON and YAML Output Formats
userStories.description: As an administrator, I want NDJSON output for SIEM integration and YAML output for configuration workflows so that I have format options for every use case.
acceptanceCriteria: --output ndjson produces one JSON object per line (Newline Delimited JSON)
NDJSON format works with all list commands
NDJSON is streamable (records emitted as received, not buffered)
--output yaml produces YAML document output
YAML output works with both list and single-resource commands
YAML lists produce YAML sequences, single resources produce YAML mappings
Output format 'ndjson' and 'yaml' registered in the output engine
Both formats respect --fields and --exclude flags
userStories.notes: NDJSON is critical for SIEM tools (Splunk, Elastic) that ingest one event per line. YAML is useful for IaC workflows. Both are simple additions to the output engine.

userStories.title: Recipe Engine Core (YAML Parser, Template Engine, Step Execution)
userStories.description: As a developer, I want a recipe engine that parses YAML recipe definitions, renders Go templates in step commands, and executes steps sequentially so that multi-step workflows can be automated.
acceptanceCriteria: Recipe YAML parsed with fields: name, description, author, version, tags, parameters, steps, on_success, on_failure
Parameters support: name, description, required, type (string/bool/int), default
Step commands rendered using Go text/template with parameter values ({{ .paramName }})
Steps executed sequentially in order defined
Step 'when' conditional evaluated (template expression returning truthy/falsy)
Step 'capture' field stores step output in a named variable for later steps
on_success.message rendered and displayed on successful completion
on_failure.message rendered with {{ .failed_step }} on failure
Recipe execution stops on first step failure (unless continue_on_error set)
Each step's command dispatched to the existing CLI command tree (not shell exec)
Recipe files loaded from ~/.config/jc/recipes/ and built-in locations
userStories.notes: The recipe engine executes jc commands internally, not via shell. This means recipes are safe, auditable, and get plan mode support. Go text/template is the template engine.

userStories.title: Built-In Recipe Library
userStories.description: As an administrator, I want 10+ built-in recipes for common workflows (onboarding, offboarding, auditing, compliance) so that I have ready-to-use automation from day one.
acceptanceCriteria: onboard-user recipe: create user, add to groups, optional device bind, send welcome email
offboard-user recipe: lock account, remove all group memberships, unbind devices, reset MFA, optional delete
audit-inactive-users recipe: find users with no login in N days, export report
audit-unmanaged-devices recipe: find devices not in any group
mfa-enforcement-check recipe: list users without MFA enabled
compliance-report recipe: generate compliance snapshot (MFA adoption, inactive users, unmanaged devices)
stale-device-cleanup recipe: find devices not seen in N days, optional delete
password-expiry-report recipe: list users with passwords expiring in N days
bulk-create-users recipe: create users from CSV file
group-sync recipe: sync group membership from CSV
All recipes have clear descriptions, documented parameters, and tested execution
Built-in recipes bundled into the binary (embedded via go:embed)
userStories.notes: Embed recipe YAML files in the binary using Go embed. Each recipe should be well-documented with parameter descriptions and example usage.

userStories.title: Recipe Commands (list, show, run, validate)
userStories.description: As an administrator, I want CLI commands to discover, inspect, run, and validate recipes so that I can use and trust the recipe system.
acceptanceCriteria: 'jc recipe list' lists all available recipes (built-in + user-defined) with name and description
'jc recipe list --output table' shows recipes in a scannable table
'jc recipe show <name>' displays full recipe details: steps, parameters, tags
'jc recipe run <name> --param value' executes a recipe with parameters
'jc recipe run <name> --plan' previews all steps without executing
'jc recipe validate <file.yaml>' validates a recipe YAML file for syntax and semantic errors
Missing required parameters produce error listing which parameters are needed
Recipe execution shows progress per step: [1/N] step-name... done
Recipe failure shows which step failed and the error
Recipe plan mode shows all steps with resolved commands
userStories.notes: Recipe commands are the user-facing interface to the recipe engine (US-042). Plan mode for recipes shows the expanded commands for each step.

userStories.title: Recipe Create, Import, and Export
userStories.description: As an administrator, I want to create new recipes interactively, import recipes from URLs or files, and export recipes for sharing so that I can build and share custom workflows.
acceptanceCriteria: 'jc recipe create' starts interactive recipe builder (name, description, parameters, steps)
'jc recipe create' outputs valid YAML to ~/.config/jc/recipes/<name>.yaml
'jc recipe import <url>' downloads and saves a recipe from a URL
'jc recipe import <path>' copies a local YAML file to the recipes directory
'jc recipe export <name>' outputs recipe YAML to stdout
'jc recipe export <name> --file output.yaml' writes recipe to specified file
Import validates the recipe before saving
Import from URL requires user confirmation (shows recipe name and steps)
Duplicate recipe name prompts for overwrite confirmation
userStories.notes: Interactive recipe builder uses survey/huh for step-by-step prompts. Import from URL should validate before saving to prevent malicious recipes.

userStories.title: MCP Server — Stdio Transport
userStories.description: As an AI integration developer, I want jc mcp serve to start an MCP server over stdio so that Claude Desktop, Claude Code, and other MCP clients can manage JumpCloud resources through tool calls.
acceptanceCriteria: 'jc mcp serve' starts MCP server on stdio transport (stdin/stdout JSON-RPC)
Server implements MCP protocol: initialize, tools/list, tools/call, resources/list, resources/read
Server reuses CLI authentication, API client, caching, and resolution engine
Server responds to initialize with server name, version, and capabilities
Server logs to stderr (not stdout) to avoid corrupting the JSON-RPC stream
Server handles Ctrl+C/SIGTERM gracefully
Claude Desktop integration works with mcpServers config: {"command": "jc", "args": ["mcp", "serve"]}
JC_PROFILE env var respected for multi-org MCP sessions
Server rate limits tool calls (configurable, default 60/minute)
All tool calls logged to ~/.config/jc/mcp-audit.log
userStories.notes: MCP uses JSON-RPC 2.0 over stdio. The server wraps the existing CLI command infrastructure. Use an established Go MCP library if available, otherwise implement the protocol directly.

userStories.title: MCP Tools — All Command Groups as Tools
userStories.description: As an AI agent, I want MCP tools for all major JumpCloud operations (users, devices, groups, insights, commands, policies, apps, recipes) so that I can fully manage JumpCloud through natural language.
acceptanceCriteria: users_list, users_get, users_create, users_update, users_delete tools registered
users_lock, users_unlock, users_reset_mfa, users_reset_password tools registered
devices_list, devices_get, devices_lock, devices_restart, devices_erase tools registered
groups_list, groups_add_member, groups_remove_member tools registered
insights_query, insights_count tools registered
commands_list, commands_run tools registered
policies_list tools registered
recipe_run tool registered
plan tool: previews any command string (plan-first safety)
explain tool: describes what a command would do
Each tool has proper JSON Schema for inputSchema describing all parameters
Destructive tools (delete, lock, erase) have destructiveHint annotation
Destructive tools default to plan mode (return plan unless execute=true)
userStories.notes: 40+ tools total. Each tool maps to a CLI command. Destructive tools are plan-first by default — the agent must explicitly pass execute: true to actually perform the action. This is the core LLM safety mechanism.

userStories.title: MCP Resources — Schema, Org Info, and Recipes
userStories.description: As an AI agent, I want MCP resources that expose JumpCloud schemas, org information, and recipe definitions so that I have context for intelligent tool usage.
acceptanceCriteria: jc://schema/users resource returns user field schema (fields, types, filters, sort_fields)
jc://schema/devices resource returns device field schema
jc://schema/groups resource returns group field schema
jc://schema/commands resource returns full CLI command manifest (all commands, flags, examples)
jc://schema/{resource} pattern works for any resource type
jc://org/info resource returns current org name, ID, user count, device count
jc://org/stats resource returns org statistics (MFA adoption, active users, etc.)
jc://recipes/list resource returns available recipes with descriptions and parameters
jc://recipes/{name} resource returns full recipe YAML definition
jc://config/profiles resource returns available profile names (no secrets)
Resources respond with proper MIME types (application/json)
userStories.notes: MCP Resources provide context that helps LLMs use tools correctly. Schema resources are especially important — they tell the LLM which fields exist and what filters are available.

userStories.title: MCP Prompts and Safety Model
userStories.description: As an AI agent, I want pre-built MCP prompts for common workflows and a safety model that prevents accidental destructive actions so that AI-assisted JumpCloud management is both capable and safe.
acceptanceCriteria: onboard_user prompt guides through complete onboarding flow
offboard_user prompt guides through offboarding with recipe preview
security_audit prompt checks MFA adoption, inactive users, auth failures
find_user_info prompt does deep user lookup (profile, groups, devices, auth events)
troubleshoot_auth prompt diagnoses authentication issues
compliance_check prompt queries across users, devices, and policies
Safety: destructive operations return plan by default (agent must confirm)
Safety: MCP audit log captures all tool calls with timestamp, tool, parameters, result
'jc mcp serve --read-only' mode disables all mutation tools
Rate limiting enforced (default 60 calls/minute, configurable)
MCP config section in config.yaml controls: transport, rate_limit, read_only, audit_log, plan_first
userStories.notes: Prompts are pre-built guidance templates. The safety model is critical — plan-first for destructive ops, audit logging, rate limiting, and read-only mode give admins control over AI agent behavior.

userStories.title: Schema System (jc schema commands)
userStories.description: As an LLM or developer, I want jc schema commands to return machine-readable descriptions of all resources, fields, and CLI commands so that tools can auto-discover the CLI's capabilities.
acceptanceCriteria: 'jc schema resources' returns list of all resource types as JSON array
'jc schema users' returns user schema: resource name, API versions, verbs, fields (name/type/description/required), filters, sort_fields
'jc schema devices' returns device schema in same format
'jc schema commands' returns full command manifest: all commands with paths, descriptions, flags (type/default/description), and examples
Schema output is always JSON (deterministic, machine-parseable)
Schema is generated from command definitions (single source of truth)
Schema includes all registered resource types dynamically
Schema includes field types: string, bool, int, datetime, array, object
userStories.notes: The schema system is what makes jc truly LLM-friendly — it provides self-describing metadata. Schema should be auto-generated from Cobra command definitions and resource type metadata.

userStories.title: Structured Machine-Readable Errors
userStories.description: As an LLM or script author, I want all errors returned as structured JSON with error codes, messages, and suggestions so that automated error handling is reliable.
acceptanceCriteria: All errors include: code (string), message (string), suggestion (string), http_status (int if applicable), api_endpoint (string if applicable)
Error codes follow pattern: RESOURCE_ERROR (e.g., USER_NOT_FOUND, AUTH_FAILED, RATE_LIMITED)
Specific exit codes: 0=success, 1=general, 2=usage, 3=auth, 4=permission, 5=rate_limit, 10=plan, 130=interrupted
Error JSON output on stderr, command output on stdout (clean separation)
--output json errors are always valid JSON (not mixed with text)
Common errors have specific codes: USER_NOT_FOUND, DEVICE_NOT_FOUND, GROUP_NOT_FOUND, INVALID_FILTER, AUTH_EXPIRED, PERMISSION_DENIED, RATE_LIMITED
Suggestion field provides actionable next step (e.g., 'Run jc auth login to re-authenticate')
Validation errors include the specific field and expected format
userStories.notes: Structured errors are essential for LLM consumption — the error code and suggestion let an LLM diagnose and recover from failures programmatically.

userStories.title: Explain Command
userStories.description: As an administrator, I want jc explain to describe what a command would do in plain English so that I can understand LLM-generated commands before running them.
acceptanceCriteria: 'jc explain "users delete jdoe"' outputs a plain-English description of the command's effects
Explanation includes: action type, target resource, affected entities, reversibility
Explanation warns about destructive or irreversible actions
Explanation mentions side effects (e.g., group removal on user delete)
Works for any valid jc command string
Invalid command string produces parsing error with suggestion
--output json returns structured explanation for LLM consumption
Explain does NOT execute the command (read-only, similar to plan mode)
userStories.notes: Explain is a lighter version of plan mode — it describes the command without making API calls. Useful for LLM-generated commands where the user wants to understand intent before even planning.

userStories.title: Conversational Mode (jc ask)
userStories.description: As an administrator, I want to type natural language queries like 'jc ask "which users haven't logged in for 90 days?"' and get translated CLI commands so that I can use JumpCloud without memorizing syntax.
acceptanceCriteria: 'jc ask "which users haven't logged in for 90 days?"' translates to and shows the equivalent jc command
Translated commands shown to user before execution (confirm_before_execute default true)
User can approve or reject the proposed command(s)
LLM provider configurable: anthropic, openai, ollama, disabled
LLM API key stored in keychain (ask.api_key_ref in config)
Schema context (jc schema commands output) sent to LLM for accurate translation
Multi-step queries translated to recipe-like sequences
Maximum commands per query configurable (default 10)
All proposed commands logged to ~/.config/jc/ask-history.log
LLM never has direct API access — only generates command strings
userStories.notes: jc ask is the conversational interface. Architecture: user input → LLM (with schema context) → proposed commands → plan mode → user confirmation → execution. The LLM is sandboxed — it can only suggest commands, not execute them.

userStories.title: User-Defined Aliases in Config
userStories.description: As an administrator, I want to define custom command aliases in my config file so that I can create shortcuts for frequently used commands.
acceptanceCriteria: Config 'aliases' section maps alias names to full command strings
Example: inactive: "users list --filter 'last_login<=2025-11-15' -t"
'jc inactive' expands to the aliased command
Aliases can include flags and arguments
Alias expansion happens before command parsing
Alias conflicts with built-in commands produce warning
'jc config set aliases.inactive "users list --filter ..."' can create aliases
Aliases visible in 'jc config view'
userStories.notes: Aliases provide personalization. They expand early in the command parsing pipeline. Built-in commands always take precedence over aliases.

userStories.title: Stdin Support for Batch Operations
userStories.description: As an administrator, I want to pipe data into jc commands via stdin so that I can compose multi-step operations using Unix pipes.
acceptanceCriteria: 'cat users.txt | jc users delete --stdin --force' reads usernames/IDs from stdin (one per line)
'jc users list --filter "suspended=true" --ids | jc users delete --stdin' pipes IDs between commands
--stdin flag enables reading from stdin
--params-stdin reads recipe parameters as JSON from stdin
Pipe detection: when stdin is not a terminal, --stdin behavior is implied for compatible commands
Empty stdin produces no error (zero items processed)
Progress shown for multi-item stdin operations
Each line from stdin processed as a separate operation
userStories.notes: Stdin support enables Unix philosophy composability. Combined with --ids output, enables powerful pipelines: list → filter → act.

userStories.title: Pipe Detection and Auto-Disable Color/Pager
userStories.description: As a script author, I want jc to automatically disable color output and pager when output is piped so that piped output is clean and machine-readable.
acceptanceCriteria: When stdout is not a TTY, color output automatically disabled
When stdout is not a TTY, pager automatically disabled
JC_NO_COLOR env var forces color off regardless of TTY
NO_COLOR env var (standard) also respected
--no-color flag explicitly disables color
Color auto-detection works correctly with | pipe, > redirect, and $() substitution
Table output adjusts column widths to unbounded when piped (no terminal width constraint)
userStories.notes: Follow the NO_COLOR standard (https://no-color.org/). Use isatty to detect terminal. This is important for scripting reliability.

userStories.title: MCP SSE Transport
userStories.description: As a remote AI integration developer, I want jc mcp serve --transport sse to start an HTTP+SSE MCP server so that remote MCP clients can connect over the network.
acceptanceCriteria: 'jc mcp serve --transport sse' starts HTTP server with SSE transport
Default port: 8080, configurable via --port or config mcp.sse_port
SSE transport implements MCP protocol over HTTP Server-Sent Events
Server responds to /sse endpoint for event stream
Server accepts POST to /message endpoint for client-to-server messages
Authentication required for SSE transport (API key header or bearer token)
CORS headers configurable for browser-based clients
TLS support via --tls-cert and --tls-key flags
userStories.notes: SSE transport enables remote MCP access. This is useful for centralized JumpCloud management where the jc binary runs on a server. Security is critical — always require authentication for network transport.

userStories.title: MCP Tool Allow/Block Lists
userStories.description: As an administrator, I want to configure which MCP tools are available using allow/block lists so that I can restrict what AI agents can do in my JumpCloud org.
acceptanceCriteria: Config mcp.allowed_tools: list of tool patterns that are allowed (whitelist)
Config mcp.blocked_tools: list of tool patterns that are blocked (blacklist)
Wildcard support: 'users_*' allows all user tools, 'devices_erase' blocks specific tool
Block list takes precedence over allow list
Blocked tool calls return MCP error response (not silently ignored)
jc mcp serve --read-only is equivalent to blocking all mutation tools
'jc mcp tools' lists available tools (respecting allow/block configuration)
Changes to allow/block lists take effect on server restart
userStories.notes: Allow/block lists give admins fine-grained control over AI agent capabilities. Example: allow read-only queries but block device erase. Wildcard patterns use glob-style matching.

userStories.title: Command Aliases (Short Forms)
userStories.description: As an administrator, I want short command aliases (jc u for users, jc d for devices, etc.) so that I can type commands faster in interactive use.
acceptanceCriteria: 'jc u' is an alias for 'jc users'
'jc d' is an alias for 'jc devices'
'jc g' is an alias for 'jc groups'
'jc i' is an alias for 'jc insights'
Verb aliases: 'ls' for 'list', 'rm' for 'delete'
'jc u ls' is equivalent to 'jc users list'
'jc u rm jdoe' is equivalent to 'jc users delete jdoe'
Aliases registered in Cobra (appear in help and completion)
Help text shows aliases: 'Aliases: u, users'
userStories.notes: Use Cobra's Aliases field on commands. These are built-in aliases distinct from user-defined aliases in config.

userStories.title: JMESPath Query Post-Processing
userStories.description: As a power user, I want to apply JMESPath expressions to command output for advanced filtering and transformation so that I can extract exactly the data I need without external tools.
acceptanceCriteria: --query flag accepts a JMESPath expression
'jc users list --query "[?department=='Engineering'].{name:username,email:email}"' filters and reshapes output
JMESPath applied after API response and field selection
JMESPath errors produce clear error with expression and position
Works with all output formats (JMESPath applied first, then formatting)
Works with all resource commands that return JSON
Empty JMESPath result returns empty array/null (not error)
userStories.notes: JMESPath is the same query language used by AWS CLI. Use the go-jmespath package. This is a power-user feature for advanced data extraction.
